questions.question: Which option includes both primary reasons for using multiple brokers in a Kafka cluster?
options: To increase message encryption speed and distribute user authentication processing.
To achieve throughput scalability (distributing partitions across brokers) and high availability (another broker takes over if one goes down so the service continues).
So that each broker can communicate with clients written in a different programming language.
To set different message retention periods per broker in order to reduce storage costs.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option 2. The content answers 'why run more than one broker?' with two reasons: scalability (distributing partitions across multiple brokers removes the I/O ceiling of a single server) and high availability (if one broker fails, another takes over). Option 1 is unrelated to encryption or authentication. Option 3 is incorrect — the number of brokers has no connection to client programming languages. Option 4 is not mentioned in the content.

questions.question: What is the most accurate description of why partitions are divided into leader and follower in Kafka, and how this works?
options: The leader is read-only and the follower is write-only, splitting roles to improve throughput.
The leader partition handles all actual reads and writes, and followers maintain replicas. If the broker holding the leader fails, one of the followers is automatically elected as the new leader.
Leader and follower store completely independent data, and consumers choose which one to read from.
Followers hold more recent data than the leader; the leader is only for storing older data.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option 2. The content states that 'one copy becomes the leader and handles all reads and writes, while the remaining copies are followers that replicate the leader's content.' It also specifies that 'if the broker holding the leader fails, one of the followers is automatically elected as the new leader.' Option 1 has the read/write roles backwards. Option 3 is incorrect — followers store the same data as replicas, not independent data. Option 4 is the opposite of reality — since followers replicate from the leader, the leader has the most current data.

questions.question: Which comparison of ZooKeeper mode and KRaft mode is correct?
options: ZooKeeper mode embeds metadata inside Kafka brokers; KRaft mode runs a separate external ZooKeeper cluster.
KRaft mode removes ZooKeeper and embeds both metadata management and controller election directly inside Kafka brokers, operating as a single system.
KRaft is the traditional approach that has been the default since Kafka 1.0.
ZooKeeper and KRaft are functionally identical — only their names differ.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option 2. The content explains that 'KRaft removes the external ZooKeeper and embeds metadata management and controller election directly inside Kafka brokers.' It has been production-ready since Kafka 3.3 and is recommended for new deployments. Option 1 has the descriptions reversed — it is ZooKeeper mode that runs a separate external cluster. Option 3 is incorrect — KRaft is the modern approach recommended for Kafka 3.3+. Option 4 is incorrect — ZooKeeper mode requires operating two separate systems, while KRaft simplifies this to a single system.

questions.question: Which option most accurately describes the sequence in which a producer sends a message to a Kafka cluster?
options: The producer sends the message to all brokers simultaneously and stores it on whichever responds first.
The producer first fetches metadata (partition leader location) from the bootstrap broker, then sends the message directly to the leader broker for that partition.
The producer sends the message directly to ZooKeeper or the controller, which then distributes it to each broker.
The consumer must send a request first before the producer can send a message.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option 2. The content describes the communication sequence as: 1) the producer connects to the bootstrap server address and fetches metadata (partition leader location) → 2) sends the message directly to the leader broker for that partition → 3) the leader assigns an offset and writes to disk → 4) followers replicate → 5) ack response. Option 1 is wrong — messages are not broadcast to all brokers simultaneously. Option 3 is wrong — messages go directly to the leader broker, not to ZooKeeper. Option 4 is wrong — producers and consumers operate independently.

questions.question:
Given the following situation, choose the most appropriate description of Kafka cluster behavior.

[Situation] Broker A was serving as the leader for a certain partition when it suddenly went down due to a failure.
options: All data for that partition is lost and the partition is deleted.
The entire cluster stops automatically and waits until an administrator manually designates a new leader.
The controller automatically elects one of the followers as the new leader, and producers and consumers reconnect to the new leader so service continues.
The producer directly selects the new leader and notifies the consumer.
questions.explanation: The correct answer is option 3. This question asks you to apply the concepts of replication and automatic leader election to a real failure scenario. The content states that 'if the broker holding the leader fails, one of the followers is automatically elected as the new leader and takes over reads and writes,' and that 'users can continue reading and writing without data loss.' Option 1 is incorrect — follower replicas exist, so there is no data loss. Option 2 is incorrect — automatic leader election is available, so no manual intervention is needed. Option 4 is incorrect — leader election is the controller's responsibility, not the producer's.
